description: Revert enums to derived Serialize: performance degraded 2.1% (199.84 → 204.09 ns). Manual impls may be faster due to specialized string handling.

asi.change: Reverted MessageKind and OperationType to derived Serialize (removed manual impls)
asi.implication: Manual impls are likely faster for these simple enums; keep them

description: Test: change #[repr(u8)] and #[inline] -> both regressed performance. Reverted to baseline state (manual impl with #[inline(always)]).

asi.change: Tried adding #[repr(u8)] and changed #[inline] to #[inline]; both degraded performance
asi.notes: Within noise; reverted to known good state

description: Reverted to baseline state (manual Serialize with match, inline(always)). Slight improvement to 199.66 ns (within noise).

asi.change: Reverted array lookup experiment; restored manual match-based Serialize
asi.note: Within expected noise floor; confirms baseline is sound.

description: Added #[repr(u8)] to MessageKind and OperationType enums. This reduced enum size and improved cache locality, giving 1.2% speedup (199.84 → 197.44 ns).

asi.change: Added #[repr(u8)] to enums while keeping match-based Serialize
asi.hypothesis: Smaller enum size improves data locality and reduces cache pressure during serialization.

description: Added Copy trait to MessageKind and OperationType enums (with repr(u8)). Gave tiny speedup (197.44 → 196.81 ns, 0.3%).

asi.hypothesis: Marking enums as Copy may improve inlining and register allocation, very small gain.

description: Reverted removal of #[serde(default)] attributes. No improvement; slight regression to 198.10 ns (vs best 196.81). These attributes only affect deserialization."

asi.change: Removed serde(default) from time_us and seq

description: Final verification: performance stable at ~197 ns. No further improvements expected with simple changes.
